result:
# The Weight of Ordinary Days

There's a Tuesday I keep thinking about. Not a dramatic one—no diagnosis, no phone call that changes everything, no standing at the edge of something literal or metaphorical. Just a Tuesday in March where the light came through the kitchen window and hit the counter in a way that made the scratches in the laminate look like a river system seen from space.

I was making coffee. The cheap kind, the pre-ground stuff that comes in a canister and smells better than it tastes. And I remember standing there, waiting for the water to heat, and being suddenly and completely aware that I was alive. Not in the inspirational-poster way. In the strange way. The way where you feel the weight of your own tongue in your mouth and hear the refrigerator humming and realize that you are a thing that exists in a world full of other things that exist, and none of you asked for it, and here you all are anyway—you and the refrigerator and the scratched counter and the light.

I think about that Tuesday because it refuses to mean anything. It resists narrative. I can't put it in a story about my life and have it serve a purpose. It's not foreshadowing. It's not the calm before a storm. It's just a moment where the veil got thin and I saw the ordinary world for what it actually is, which is extraordinary and inexplicable and absolutely ordinary all at the same time.

---

We spend so much time waiting for our lives to start. I don't mean that in the cliché sense, though the cliché exists because it's true. I mean we have this persistent feeling that the current moment is a rough draft. That we're in rehearsal. That the real performance is coming and when it arrives we'll finally be the version of ourselves that knows what to say at dinner parties, that doesn't leave laundry in the dryer for three days, that reads the books on the nightstand instead of adding more to the pile.

But there is no performance. There's just this—the rehearsal that never ends, that *is* the thing itself. Every fumbled line, every awkward silence, every morning where you sit on the edge of the bed and need a full ninety seconds before you can stand up. That's not the preamble. That's the show.

I find this terrifying and also the most liberating thought I've ever had.

---

My grandmother used to say that God lives in the details, which I later learned was a misquotation of an architect, which I even later learned might not have been said by that architect either. It doesn't matter. The point stands regardless of who made it: the small things are where the truth hides.

Not the grand gestures. Not the declarations. The small things.

The way someone moves your shoes from the doorway to the closet without mentioning it. The way a dog sighs when it finally settles into its spot on the couch—that deep, shuddering exhale, like it has been holding its breath all day and can finally stop. The sound of a basketball on asphalt two streets over on a summer evening, irregular as a heartbeat during a dream.

I've started collecting these. Not writing them down—that would formalize them, turn them into material, and they're not material. They're just things I let myself notice. A catalog of evidence that the world is paying attention to itself, even when we're not.

---

Here is what I think I'm trying to say:

We are all walking around with this unbearable and gorgeous knowledge that everything is temporary. The coffee will go cold. The light will move off the counter. The Tuesday will become Wednesday. And we manage, most of the time, to function anyway. To make the coffee. To drive to work. To ask someone how their weekend was and genuinely care about the answer.

That's not denial. That's the bravest thing I can imagine.

There's a particular kind of courage in the person who knows—really *knows*, in the body and not just the intellect—that this will all end, and still gets up to make breakfast. Still calls their mother back. Still plants tomatoes in April even though the frost might come. Especially because the frost might come.

---

I don't have a conclusion. Conclusions are for arguments, and this isn't an argument. This is just a person standing in a kitchen on a Tuesday, trying to find the words for a feeling that might not have words, and settling for the ones that come closest.

The light moved. The coffee was fine. I drank it standing up, looking out the window at nothing in particular—the neighbor's fence, a power line, a sky that couldn't decide if it was gray or blue and so was both.

And that was enough. Not enough in the way that means you stop wanting. Enough in the way that means you realize that this moment, this one right here, with its scratched counters and its mediocre coffee and its indecisive sky, is not a rough draft of your life.

It is your life.

And it is, against all odds and expectations, full.
